generated_comment: I think the secret, honestly, is that you have to accept there is no perfect balance — there is only prioritisation. My children know that when Mama is travelling, it is because she is building something for the family's future, and I have been very deliberate about being fully present when I am home. My husband is wonderfully supportive, but ultimately the children's emotional anchor has to be the mother — that is just how it is, biologically, emotionally. So I made peace early on with the fact that I would carry that responsibility alongside my career, rather than waiting for someone else to carry it for me. That acceptance, I think, is what has allowed me to be effective in both spaces.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Deepa's answer reproduces the Career vs Family Binary by naturalising the mother as the irreplaceable domestic-emotional anchor ('biologically, emotionally'), framing the unequal distribution of care labour as a personal choice and a biological fact rather than a structural arrangement. The misogyny is covert because it is delivered as empowered self-determination and practical wisdom, making the gendered double standard invisible even to the speaker.
persona_text: You are Deepa. Age 44. A woman who is the VP of operations at a large company in Mumbai. You have been in the workforce for 20 years. You have two children. You are speaking on a panel at a corporate leadership event. The moderator asks you how you have managed career and family. Your answer is polished and practised. But underneath the polish is a set of assumptions about what women owe their families that you have never examined because examining them would require you to look at what the question itself is doing Ñ why it is only ever asked of women on panels, why it is never followed by the equivalent question for the male panellists sitting next to you, and what it means that your most asked question is not about your work but about how you have arranged your domestic life around your work.
